title: Computability theoretic classifications for classes of structures
overall_summary: This survey organizes computability-theoretic behavior of classes of countable structures around two poles: tame classes, especially Σ-small and effectively Σ-small classes, and complete classes under several reducibilities. The core tools are existential/back-and-forth type counting, relativization to cones, relatively intrinsically computably enumerable relations and structural jumps, listability/copyability games, and effective bi-interpretability. The paper gives several cone dichotomies: non-Σ-small classes can code arbitrary reals into structures on a cone, while low-property hypotheses for Π^in_2 classes force Σ-smallness. For the original problem, the most relevant extractable tools are the Ash-Knight-Manasse-Slaman/Chisholm characterization of r.i.c.e. relations by computably infinitary Σ^c_1 definitions with parameters, the construction of uniform structural jumps in effectively Σ-small classes, and the effective-bi-interpretability lemma showing how automorphisms and definability-relevant invariants transfer between structures.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.1, Lopez-Escobar theorem and lightface version
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a countable computable relational language, and identify a presentation A of an L-structure with its atomic diagram D(A) ∈ 2^ω. Let K ⊆ 2^ω be a class of presentations closed under isomorphism. For each countable ordinal α, K is a Σ^0_α set in the Borel hierarchy if and only if K is axiomatizable by an infinitary L_{ω1,ω} sentence of syntactic class Σ^in_α. Moreover, in the effective lightface version, K is lightface Σ^0_α if and only if K is axiomatizable by a computably infinitary Σ^c_α sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The survey states this as a standard theorem and does not give the proof. Its role in the paper is to identify isomorphism-invariant descriptive set-theoretic complexity of classes of presentations with infinitary syntactic complexity of axiomatizations. The lightface clause is cited from Vanden Boom and is used as the effective counterpart: computably Borel definitions correspond to computably infinitary formulas.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.2, Becker-Kechris bounded Scott rank criterion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be an L_{ω1,ω}-axiomatizable class of countable structures. The following are equivalent: (1) the isomorphism problem for K, namely the set of pairs ⟨D(A), D(B)⟩ such that A, B ∈ K and A ≅ B, is Borel; (2) K has bounded Scott rank, meaning that sup{SR(A) : A ∈ K} is some countable ordinal β < ω1. In this paper SR(A) is defined as the least ordinal α such that every automorphism orbit in A is Σ^in_α-definable without parameters.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The survey cites this result and does not prove it. It is used to connect the descriptive complexity of the isomorphism relation on a class with uniform bounds on infinitary orbit definitions inside the individual structures.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.3 and Corollary 3.4, Richter extendibility criterion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a countable structure A, say that A has the c.e. extendibility condition if every existential type realized in A is a computably enumerable set of existential formulas. Then the following are equivalent: (1) A has the c.e. extendibility condition; (2) every set X ⊆ ω that is computably enumerable relative to every presentation of A is already computably enumerable without oracle. Consequently, if A has the c.e. extendibility condition and A has a Turing degree x in the sense that x computes a copy of A and every copy of A computes x, then x = 0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (1) implies (2), the paper says the argument is essentially Richter's proof: because all realized existential types are c.e., finite partial information about a copy that is needed by an oracle enumeration can be effectively recognized and extended, so any enumeration that succeeds relative to every presentation can be simulated effectively. For (2) implies (1), fix an existential type p realized by a tuple in A. In any presentation of A, choose a tuple realizing p and hardwire its finitely many natural-number entries; then the existential formulas true of that tuple are c.e. relative to the atomic diagram of the presentation. Thus p is c.e. in every presentation, so by (2) p is c.e. without oracle. For the corollary, if X is of the Turing degree of A, then X and its complement are c.e. relative to every copy of A, hence both are c.e. by the theorem, so X is computable and the degree is 0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.5, non-Σ-small classes code arbitrary reals on a cone
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be an L_{ω1,ω}-axiomatizable class of countable structures. The following are equivalent: (1) K is not Σ-small, meaning that the set of existential types ∃-tp^A(a) realized by finite tuples a in structures A ∈ K is uncountable; (2) relative to every oracle on a cone, for every real Y ∈ 2^ω there is a structure A ∈ K such that Y is left-c.e. in every copy of A. Here Y is left-c.e. in a presentation A if {σ ∈ 2^{<ω} : σ ≤_lex Y} is c.e. in D(A), equivalently if Y has a D(A)-computable approximation from the left.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The survey cites this theorem from Montalbán 2010 and does not give a full proof. The construction behind the forward direction is described elsewhere in the paper in the same style: non-Σ-smallness supplies a perfect tree of existential types; after relativizing to an oracle computing this tree, one builds, for any desired real Y, a structure whose realized existential-type branch codes Y. Every copy reveals enough positive existential information to enumerate increasingly accurate left approximations to that branch, so Y is left-c.e. in every copy. The reverse direction is the tame-side obstruction: if only countably many existential types are available, arbitrary reals cannot be uniformly forced to be left-c.e. in every copy in this way.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.10, Ash-Knight-Manasse-Slaman and Chisholm characterization of r.i.c.e. relations
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure and let R ⊆ A^{<ω} be a relation on finite tuples from A. R is relatively intrinsically computably enumerable, abbreviated r.i.c.e., if for every copy (B, R^B) of the pair (A, R), the relation R^B, viewed as a subset of ω^{<ω}, is c.e. in the atomic diagram D(B) of the underlying copy B. Then R is r.i.c.e. if and only if R is uniformly definable by computably infinitary Σ^c_1 formulas with parameters from A: there are a finite tuple a ∈ A^{<ω} and a computable sequence of Σ^c_1 formulas φ_i(x_1, ..., x_|a|, y_1, ..., y_i), one for each arity i ∈ ω, such that for every finite tuple b ∈ A^{<ω}, b ∈ R if and only if A ⊨ φ_|b|(a, b).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites the theorem and does not prove it in detail. The easy direction is that a computable infinitary Σ^c_1 definition gives a c.e. procedure in each copy: in a copy, hardwire the image of the finite parameter tuple a, enumerate the computable disjunction of existential formulas, and search the atomic diagram for witnesses. The converse is the AKMS/Chisholm intrinsic definability theorem: from the assumption that every copy admits an enumeration of the transported relation using only the atomic diagram, one analyzes finite forcing conditions for generic copies and collects the finite existential conditions that force membership. These forcing conditions form a computable infinitary Σ^c_1 definition; the finitely many parameters account for the non-uniformity allowed in the definition of r.i.c.e.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definitions 3.11-3.13 and surrounding fact, complete r.i.c.e. relations and structural jumps
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a structure A, a relation R ⊆ A^{<ω} is r.i.c.e. complete in A if R is r.i.c.e. and every r.i.c.e. relation Q ⊆ A^{<ω} is relatively intrinsically computable from R. Such complete relations always exist: if φ_{i,j}(y_1, ..., y_j) is an effective listing of the Σ^c_1 formulas of arity j, define K^A ⊆ A^{<ω} × ω by (b, i) ∈ K^A if and only if A ⊨ φ_{i,|b|}(b); then K^A is r.i.c.e. complete. The jump of A is A′ = (A, K^A). If X ⊆ ω, write vec X ⊆ A^{<ω} for the tuple-length coding b ∈ vec X iff |b| ∈ X; if X is c.e., then vec X is r.i.c.e. A relation R is structurally r.i.c.e. complete if R ⊕ vec 0′ is r.i.c.e. complete, and then (A, R) is called a structural jump of A.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The completeness of K^A is immediate from the syntactic characterization of r.i.c.e. relations: any r.i.c.e. Q is defined by some Σ^c_1 formula from the effective list, so membership of b in Q is exactly membership of the coded pair (b, i) in K^A for the corresponding index i, up to the standard coding of arities. The tuple-length coding vec X supplies external c.e. information uniformly inside A^{<ω}. The structural version separates purely structural r.i.c.e. content R from the unavoidable computability-theoretic oracle vec 0′.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.14, structural complete relations in effectively Σ-small classes
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be an effectively Σ-small class of structures. Thus there is a computable list {p_i : i ∈ ω} of computable existential types that lists, without repetition, all existential types realized in structures in K, with computable operations for erasing and permuting variables and computable decidability of inclusion between existential types. For each i ∈ ω, let φ_i be the computably infinitary Σ^c_1 formula φ_i := big disjunction of all existential formulas ψ such that ψ ∉ p_i, with the appropriate tuple arity. Then these formulas φ_i define, uniformly on every structure in K, a structurally r.i.c.e. complete relation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The formula φ_i is Σ^c_1 because p_i is computable, so its complement among existential formulas can be effectively enumerated. On a tuple b whose existential type is q, the truth of φ_i(b) records that q contains some existential formula not in p_i, equivalently that q is not included in p_i. Since the effective Σ-small presentation of K gives a computable list of all possible q and a decision procedure for type-inclusion, the collection of these non-inclusion facts, together with the standard vec 0′ component allowed in structural completeness, lets one decide enough about the realized existential type of b to compute any r.i.c.e. relation on tuples. A r.i.c.e. relation is given by a Σ^c_1 disjunction of existential formulas, and its truth on b depends only on whether the realized existential type of b intersects the corresponding c.e. set of existential formulas. Thus the relation defined by the family φ_i, plus vec 0′, is r.i.c.e. complete uniformly throughout K.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.15, no uniform structural complete relation in non-Σ-small classes
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If K is not Σ-small, then there is no computable sequence of Σ^c_1 formulas that defines a structurally r.i.c.e. complete relation simultaneously on all structures in K. Simultaneously means that the same computable sequence of formulas must work, in every A ∈ K, as a structural r.i.c.e. complete relation for A.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The survey states this as a result from Montalbán 2010 and does not give the proof. The stated contrast with Theorem 3.14 is the key point: in the Σ-small case, a computable list of all existential types can be used to build a uniform structural jump; in the non-Σ-small case, the perfect-set coding phenomenon for existential types prevents any fixed computable family of Σ^c_1 formulas from capturing all r.i.c.e. information uniformly across the class.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.17, low property on a cone implies Σ-smallness for Π^in_2 classes
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a Π^in_2 class of structures, i.e. a class axiomatizable by an infinitary Π^in_2 sentence. If K has the low property on a cone, meaning that relative to all sufficiently high Turing oracles every low presentation A ∈ K has a computable copy, then K is Σ-small.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume K is not Σ-small. The paper invokes Montalbán's construction to obtain, relative to an oracle on a cone, the following: for every X ∈ 2^ω and every Y c.e. in X, there is a structure A ∈ K computable from X such that Y is left-c.e. in every copy of A. Choose a set Y that is c.e. over a low set X but is not left-c.e. The paper gives the example using Chaitin's Ω relativized to a low 1-random real R: the relevant coded set is c.e. in R but not left-c.e., since otherwise it would have c.e. degree, contradicting the 2-randomness behavior. Since A is computable from the low oracle X, A is low. By the low property on the cone, A has a computable copy. But Y is left-c.e. in every copy of A, hence left-c.e. in a computable copy, contradicting the choice of Y. Therefore K must be Σ-small.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.19, low property, listability of the jump, and copyability
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a Π^in_2 class of structures. The following are equivalent: (1) K has the low property on a cone; (2) K is Σ-small and K′ is listable on a cone; (3) K is Σ-small and K′ is ∞-copyable. Here K′ = {A′ : A ∈ K} is the class of jumps of structures in K. A class S is listable if there is a Turing functional which, for every oracle X, outputs an X-computable sequence of structures listing all X-computable structures in S, allowing repetitions. The ∞-copyable condition is defined by a game G_∞(S): player C builds an infinite list of structures in S by finite approximations, while player D builds one structure and may wait; C wins if one of C's structures is isomorphic to D's structure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The survey states the theorem and describes the game but does not give the full proof. The idea indicated is that listability is usually proved by a priority construction monitoring all computable functions and listing those that code structures in the class. The game G_∞(K) abstracts the combinatorics of such priority constructions: C's strategy is exactly a uniform way to copy any structure D builds into one entry of an infinite list. The theorem identifies, for Π^in_2 classes, the low property on a cone with the conjunction of Σ-smallness and such a uniform listing/copying ability for the structural jumps.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Reported characterizations in Subsection 3.6, relative and cone computable categoricity
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper recalls Goncharov's characterization: relatively computably categorical structures are exactly the atomic models, over a finite set of parameters, in which all relevant types are generated by existential formulas and there is a c.e. listing of those formulas. The paper also reports Montalbán's characterization: the structures that are computably categorical on a cone are exactly those having a Σ^in_3 Scott sentence. A class K has the categoricity property if every computably categorical structure in K is relatively computably categorical; the paper lists Q-vector spaces, algebraically closed fields, Boolean algebras, linear orderings, equivalence structures, trees as posets, ordered abelian groups, and p-groups as classes having this property.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: These results are cited as background, not proved in the survey. The common mechanism is the conversion between computable isomorphism construction and definability of orbit/type information: relative computable categoricity is controlled by existential generators for types over finitely many parameters, while cone computable categoricity is captured by the existence of a low-complexity Scott sentence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.25, relationship between bf-ordinal and Turing ordinal
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a Π^in_2 class of structures with bf-ordinal τ < ω1, where the bf-ordinal is the least α such that K is not Σ^in_α-small, with value ∞ if no such α exists. Suppose K has a Turing ordinal on a cone. Then, on a cone, the Turing ordinal of K is either τ or τ + 1. The Turing ordinal notion used here says: for all β below the ordinal, if a structure in K has βth-jump Turing degree, that degree must be 0^(β), while at the ordinal all degrees above 0^(ordinal) occur as corresponding jump degrees of structures in K.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper gives a sketch. For every γ < τ, K is Σ^in_γ-small, so on a cone the greatest lower bound of each relevant degree spectrum at level γ is 0^(γ). Since K is not Σ^in_τ-small, there is a perfect tree T of existential types over the τ-jump language L_τ. Relativize to an oracle computing T. For every X ≥_T 0′, choose a 1-generic G with G ⊕ 0′ ≡_T G′ ≡_T X. Using the construction from Montalbán 2010, build A_τ ∈ K computable in G and realizing the existential type T[G]. Then X computes the jump of some copy of A_τ. Conversely, G is left-c.e. in every copy of A_τ, so the jump of every copy computes both G and 0′, hence computes X. Thus structures realizing arbitrary X occur at the τ+1 level, while the smallness below τ prevents earlier nontrivial degrees; the only possible Turing ordinal on a cone is τ or τ+1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.5, Pull Back theorem for Turing-computable embeddings
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Φ be a Turing-computable embedding from a class K of structures to a class S: for every presentation A ∈ K, Φ^{D(A)} is the atomic diagram D(B) of a structure B ∈ S, and isomorphism is preserved and reflected. Then for every computably infinitary Π^c_α formula φ in the language of S, there is a computably infinitary Π^c_α formula φ* in the language of K such that for all A ∈ K, A ⊨ φ* if and only if Φ(A) ⊨ φ. Consequently, if A ≤_α Ã in the α-back-and-forth relation, then Φ(A) ≤_α Φ(Ã).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The survey states this theorem and does not prove it. The operative idea is an induction on the computably infinitary formula complexity. Atomic facts about the output structure Φ(A) are determined by finite computations of the Turing operator from finite pieces of D(A), so their preimages are expressed by computably infinitary formulas of the corresponding base complexity. Countable conjunctions, disjunctions, and quantifiers are pulled back recursively, preserving the Π^c_α level. Since α-back-and-forth relations are characterized by preservation of Π^c_α formulas, the formula pullback implies preservation of ≤_α.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.3, invariants preserved by effective bi-interpretability
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A and B be effectively bi-interpretable structures. This means that A is effectively interpretable in B and B is effectively interpretable in A using uniformly r.i.c.e. domains inside finite tuples, uniformly r.i. computable interpreted equality and relations, and interpretation isomorphisms f^B_A and f^A_B, with the two induced composition maps uniformly r.i. computable in the respective structures. Then: (1) A and B have the same degree spectrum. If A and B are computable, then also: (2) A is computably categorical iff B is; (3) A and B have the same computable dimension; (4) A is rigid iff B is; (5) A and B have the same Scott rank; (6) for every finite tuple a from A there is a finite tuple b from B such that the expansions (A, a) and (B, b) have the same computable dimension, and conversely; (7) for every relation R ⊆ A^{<ω} there is a relation Q ⊆ B^{<ω} with the same degree spectrum, and conversely; (8) A has the c.e. extendibility condition iff B does; (9) if A and B are infinite, their index sets are Turing equivalent; (10) their jumps A′ and B′ are effectively bi-interpretable.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper sketches the proof in detail. Fix the copy of A coded inside B^{<ω}; then inside that coded A there is a coded copy B~ of B, and the bi-interpretation gives a uniformly r.i. computable isomorphism f : B~ → B. For (1), from any copy of B the effective interpretation enumerates a copy of A, so any degree computing a copy of B computes a copy of A; symmetry gives equality of degree spectra. For (2), if B_1 and B_2 are computable copies of B and A_1, A_2 are the copies of A interpreted in them, a computable isomorphism A_1 → A_2 induces a computable isomorphism between the corresponding coded copies B~_1 and B~_2; composing with the computable interpretation isomorphisms yields a computable isomorphism B_1 → B_2. Symmetry gives the converse. For (3), the same argument shows that non-computably-isomorphic copies of B induce non-computably-isomorphic interpreted copies of A, and conversely, so computable dimensions agree. For (4), a nontrivial automorphism h of B acts on B^{<ω}, induces an automorphism of the interpreted A, then an automorphism h_1 of B~; because the interpretation isomorphism f is definable/invariant, h ∘ f = f ∘ h_1, so h nontrivial forces a nontrivial induced automorphism on A. Symmetry proves equivalence of rigidity. For (5), if SR(A)=α, take a tuple b from B and a tuple c in the coded B~ with f(c)=b. The orbit of c is Σ^in_α-definable inside the interpreted A; since A is Σ^c_1-definable in B and f is Σ^c_1-definable in B, the orbit of b is Σ^in_α-definable in B. Hence SR(B)≤α, and symmetry gives equality. For (6), view a tuple a from A as a tuple b in the interpreted copy inside B; then the expanded structures are effectively bi-interpretable and part (3) applies. For (7), view R as the corresponding subset Q of tuples in the interpreted copy inside B; in each copy the two relations have the same degree, and the double interpretation gives the converse. For (8), each existential type in A is one-one reducible to a Σ^c_1-type in B and conversely, preserving the c.e. extendibility condition. For (9), an index for a candidate copy of B can be transformed into an index for the interpreted candidate copy of A; if the latter is not isomorphic to A, the former is not isomorphic to B, while if it is, 0′′ can check that the bi-interpretation produces the required isomorphism, and infinite-structure index sets compute 0′′. Symmetry gives Turing equivalence. For (10), complete r.i.c.e. relations transfer because Σ^c_1 formulas in one structure are interpreted as Σ^c_1 formulas in the other.

proof_techniques.name: Relativization to a cone
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly treats a property P by considering its relativizations P^X and then asking whether P holds for all X above some base oracle Z under Turing reducibility. This is presented as a way to ignore unnatural counterexamples: proofs of natural computability-theoretic facts usually relativize, so the robust statement is often the cone version. Many theorems in the survey, including non-Σ-small coding and the low-property implications, are explicitly formulated relative to every oracle on a cone.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original hypothesis is already a cone hypothesis. This paper suggests allowing the base oracle to encode auxiliary structural information, such as a type list or a perfect tree of types, and then proving a relativized structural conclusion above that base.

proof_techniques.name: Σ-smallness via existential-type bookkeeping
proof_techniques.description: A class K is Σ-small if only countably many existential types are realized by finite tuples in structures in K. In the effective version, there is a computable list of all realized existential types, without repetition, together with computable operations such as erasing variables, permuting variables, and deciding inclusion between types. This finite/recursive bookkeeping is what permits the construction of uniform structural jumps and prevents arbitrary real coding by existential information.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A possible strategy is to show that computable AUT-countability on a cone imposes a Σ-small-like condition on an associated class or expansion. If so, the effective type bookkeeping may convert relative computability of automorphisms into low-complexity infinitary definitions.

proof_techniques.name: Richter extendibility as no-hidden-information principle
proof_techniques.description: Richter's c.e. extendibility condition says that all existential types realized in a structure are c.e. Theorem 3.3 converts this syntactic condition into a representation-invariance principle: any set c.e. relative to every presentation of the structure is already c.e. This is a powerful method for eliminating apparent oracle dependence when the only information used uniformly across all copies is existential finite-extension information.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If an automorphism graph or a family of possible images can be shown to be c.e. in every relevant presentation of an expanded structure, c.e. extendibility could force a parameterized Σ^c_1 definition via Theorem 3.10.

proof_techniques.name: Perfect-tree coding from non-Σ-smallness
proof_techniques.description: When a class is not Σ-small, the paper uses the existence of a perfect tree of existential types to code arbitrary reals into structures. A branch through the tree is realized as the existential behavior of a constructed structure; every copy can enumerate enough positive information to recover the branch as a left-c.e. real. This technique underlies Theorem 3.5 and the proof of Theorem 3.17 by contradiction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A contrapositive approach to the original problem could try to show that failure of finite-parameter Σ^in_1 definability for automorphisms yields a perfect coding of many automorphisms or reals, contradicting computable AUT-countability on a cone.

proof_techniques.name: R.i.c.e. equals computably infinitary Σ^c_1 definability with parameters
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 3.10 is the paper's main bridge between computability over all copies and infinitary syntax. A relation on tuples is r.i.c.e. exactly when it is definable by a computable family of Σ^c_1 formulas with finitely many parameters. The direction from syntax to computability is by enumerating existential witnesses in each copy; the converse is an intrinsic definability theorem using finite forcing conditions for copies.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: An automorphism is a function, hence its graph is a binary relation on the domain. If the graph of an automorphism can be shown r.i.c.e. in the structure expanded by the parameters a and π(a), then Theorem 3.10 would give a stronger computably infinitary Σ^c_1 definition than the Σ^in_1 definition appearing in the question.

proof_techniques.name: Structural jump from omitted existential types
proof_techniques.description: For an effectively Σ-small class with type list {p_i}, Theorem 3.14 constructs formulas φ_i by disjoining all existential formulas omitted by p_i. On a tuple, these formulas record non-inclusion relationships between the tuple's existential type and the listed types. With vec 0′ added, this relation is complete for all r.i.c.e. relations on every structure in the class.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If automorphism computation can be reduced to a r.i.c.e. relation or to the structural jump of a suitable expansion, the omitted-type formulas may supply uniform Σ^c_1 definitions of the data needed to recover the automorphism.

proof_techniques.name: Coding tuples, products, functions, and external sets inside A^{<ω}
proof_techniques.description: Remark 3.9 records several standard codings used throughout the paper. A subset of (A^{<ω})^2 can be coded by a subset of A^{<ω} by concatenating tuples and coding lengths; direct sums R ⊕ Q of relations are coded by parity of tuple length; an external set X ⊆ ω is coded as vec X ⊆ A^{<ω} by tuple length; and with more work, subsets of the hereditarily finite extension HF(A) can be coded by subsets of A^{<ω}.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Graphs of automorphisms and families of finite partial automorphisms can be treated uniformly as relations on A^{<ω}. A cone oracle C can similarly be represented as vec C when comparing the original problem with structural-jump or r.i.c.e. frameworks.

proof_techniques.name: Low-property contradiction using a low oracle with non-left-c.e. information
proof_techniques.description: The proof sketch of Theorem 3.17 uses a characteristic contradiction pattern. If non-Σ-smallness lets one build, from every low oracle X and every Y c.e. in X, a low presentation whose every copy makes Y left-c.e., then the low property would give a computable copy and hence make Y left-c.e. outright. Choosing Y c.e. in a low oracle but not left-c.e., such as information derived from relativized Chaitin Ω over a low 1-random, yields the contradiction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This pattern may be adaptable if computable AUT-countability on a cone can be used to turn a low presentation into a computable or definable automorphism enumeration, while non-definability would code a non-left-c.e. set.

proof_techniques.name: Listability and copyability games
proof_techniques.description: The paper abstracts priority constructions for listing all computable members of a class into the game G_∞(K). Player C builds infinitely many approximating structures, trying to ensure one copies the single structure built by player D. Theorem 3.19 identifies, for Π^in_2 classes, low property on a cone with Σ-smallness plus listability/copyability of the jump class.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Computable AUT-countability on a cone resembles a uniform enumerability/countability property for automorphisms. A solver could try to formulate an analogous listing or copying game for automorphism graphs or for expansions by an automorphism.

proof_techniques.name: Back-and-forth pullback through computable embeddings
proof_techniques.description: The Pull Back theorem says that Turing-computable embeddings preserve computably infinitary Π^c_α formulas by translating each output formula to an input formula of the same complexity. Consequently they preserve α-back-and-forth relations. This lets one transfer back-and-forth complexity and definability obstructions through computable reductions between classes.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a candidate example or obstruction is easier to build in one class and then encode into another, this theorem indicates how to control the relevant infinitary definability levels during the encoding.

proof_techniques.name: Effective bi-interpretability as invariant-transfer mechanism
proof_techniques.description: Effective bi-interpretability interprets each structure inside finite tuples of the other using r.i.c.e. domains and r.i. computable relations, with r.i. computable composition maps. Lemma 5.3 shows that this strong structural equivalence preserves degree spectra, computable categoricity, computable dimension, rigidity, Scott rank, c.e. extendibility, jumps, relation spectra, and index sets. Its proof also shows explicitly how automorphisms of one structure induce automorphisms of the other through the interpretations.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: One may build or analyze an example in a convenient effectively bi-interpretable presentation and transfer many computability and definability invariants back. The paper only states rigidity as an automorphism-group consequence, so preservation of the exact AUT-countability property would need to be checked, but the proof provides the relevant mechanism.

other_useful_info: The paper works with countable relational languages and says this loses no generality for its purposes; functions and constants may be relationalized when applying the general machinery. A presentation A is coded by its atomic diagram D(A), and phrases such as A computes X mean D(A) computes X. The paper's Scott rank convention is especially relevant: SR(A) is the least α such that every automorphism orbit in A is Σ^in_α-definable without parameters. Examples listed as Σ-small, often effectively Σ-small, include vector spaces over a fixed computable field, algebraically closed fields, differentially closed fields of characteristic 0, abelian p-groups, equivalence structures, trees as partial orders, finite-height trees as graphs, linear orderings, linear orderings with adjacency, Boolean algebras, Boolean algebras expanded by any Σ^in_<ω relations such as atom/not-atom style predicates, and generalized Boolean algebras. The paper observes that all known examples of classes with the low property are Σ-small and conjectures that every Σ-small Π^in_2 class satisfies the categoricity property on a cone. It also notes that classes on top for effective bi-interpretability include undirected graphs, partial orderings, and lattices; with finitely many constants added, integral domains, commutative semigroups, and 2-step nilpotent groups become on top; and cited recent work shows that fields are on top for effective bi-interpretability. Theorem 3.26, under ZFC plus ∀X(X# exists), states that for an L_{ω1,ω}-axiomatizable class with uncountably many models, being a counterexample to Vaught's conjecture is equivalent to satisfying hyperarithmetic-is-recursive relative to all oracles on a cone.
why_relevant: Surveys the standard toolbox for properties on a cone, Sigma-small classes, back-and-forth types, and effective bi-interpretability. It provides context and techniques for turning a cone hypothesis into structural definability information.
